revision.text:
[[ar:شروق نجمي]][[bg:Хелиакален изгрев]][[ca:Ortus helíac]][[de:Heliakisch]][[el:Ηλιακή ανατολή]][[en:Heliacal rising]][[eo:Helioapero]][[es:Orto helíaco]][[fa:کنار روزی]][[fi:Heliakkinen nousu]][[fr:Lever héliaque]][[ga:Éirí héileacach]][[gl:Orto helíaco]][[ig:Uto Heliacal]][[it:Levata eliaca]][[ja:ヒライアカル・ライジング]][[ko:신출]][[la:Ortus et occasus heliacus]][[nn:Heliakisk oppgang]][[no:Heliakisk oppgang]][[pl:Wschód heliakalny]][[pt:Nascer helíaco]][[ro:Răsărit heliacal]][[ru:Гелиакический восход]][[sk:Heliaktický východ hviezdy]][[sv:Heliakisk uppgång]][[ta:விண்மீன் எழுச்சி]][[uk:Геліактичний схід]][[vi:Mọc cùng Mặt Trời]][[zh:偕日升]] {{Short description|Rising of stars prior to sunrise}} The '''heliacal rising''' ({{IPAc-en|h|ɪ|ˈ|l|aɪ|.|ə|k|əl}} {{respell|hih|LY|ə|kəl}}){{cite OED|heliacal}}{{cite Merriam-Webster|heliacal}}{{cite Dictionary.com|heliacal}} of a {{ill|Star|en|Star|qid=Q523|lt=Star|1=Star}} or a {{ill|Planet|en|Planet|qid=Q634|lt=Planet|1=Planet}} occurs annually when it becomes visible above the eastern {{ill|Horizon|en|Horizon|qid=Q43261|lt=Horizon|1=Horizon}} at {{ill|dawn|en|dawn|qid=Q11326182|lt=dawn|1=Dawn}} just before {{ill|Sunrise|en|Sunrise|qid=Q193294|lt=Sunrise|1=Sunrise}} (thus becoming "the [[Morning Star (disambiguation)#Astronomy|morning star]]").{{cite web |url=ttp://solar-center.stanford.edu/AO/dawn-rising.html |title=Show Me a Dawn, or "Heliacal," Rising |publisher=[[Stanford University]]|quote=Heliacal risings occur after a star has been behind the Sun for a season and it is just returning to visibility. There is one morning, just before dawn, when the star suddenly reappears after its absence. On that day it "blinks" on for a moment just before the sunrise and just before it is then obliterated by the Sun's presence. That one special morning is called the star's heliacal rising.}} A heliacal rising marks the time when a star or planet becomes visible for the first time again in the {{ill|night sky|en|night sky|qid=Q1153471|lt=night sky|1=Night sky}} after having set with the Sun at the western horizon in a previous {{ill|Sunset|en|Sunset|qid=Q166564|lt=Sunset|1=Sunset}} (its '''heliacal setting'''), having since been in the {{ill|Sky|en|Sky|qid=Q527|lt=Sky|1=Sky}} only during {{ill|daytime|en|daytime|qid=Q52643|lt=daytime|1=Daytime}}, obscured by sunlight. Historically, the most important such rising is that of {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sirius|1=Sirius}}, which was an important feature of the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|qid=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar|1=Egyptian calendar}} and {{ill|Egyptian astronomy|en|Egyptian astronomy|qid=Q751843|lt=astronomical development|1=Egyptian astronomy}}. The rising of the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|qid=Q13423|lt=Pleiades|1=Pleiades}} heralded the start of the {{ill|Ancient Greek|en|Ancient Greek|qid=Q35497|lt=Ancient Greek|1=Ancient Greek}} sailing season, using {{ill|celestial navigation|en|celestial navigation|qid=Q1063512|lt=celestial navigation|1=Celestial navigation}},{{cite OED|Pleiad}} as well as the farming season (attested by {{ill|Hesiod|en|Hesiod|qid=Q44233|lt=Hesiod|1=Hesiod}} in his {{ill|Works and Days|en|Works and Days|qid=Q667285|lt=Works and Days|1=Works and Days}}). Heliacal rising is one of several types of risings and settings, mostly they are grouped into morning and evening risings and settings of objects in the sky. {{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|qid=Q611114|lt=Culmination|1=Culmination}} in the evening and then morning is set apart by half a year, while on the other hand risings and settings in the evenings and the mornings are only at the equator set apart by half a year. == Cause and significance == [[File:Kepler_Observatory_Linz_under_the_Stars.jpg|thumb|Sirius is the fixed star with the greatest {{ill|apparent magnitude|en|apparent magnitude|qid=Q124313|lt=apparent magnitude|1=Apparent magnitude}} and one which is almost non-variable. The {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|qid=Q13423|lt=Pleiades|1=Pleiades}}, a key feature of {{ill|Taurus (constellation)|en|Taurus (constellation)|qid=Q10570|lt=Taurus|1=Taurus (constellation)}} shown across {{ill|Orion (constellation)|en|Orion (constellation)|qid=Q8860|lt=Orion|1=Orion (constellation)}} in the same photograph also experience an annual period of visibility ("rising and setting"). Photo taken at sunset.]] Relative to the stars, the Sun appears to drift eastward about one degree per day along a path called the {{ill|Ecliptic|en|Ecliptic|qid=Q79852|lt=Ecliptic|1=Ecliptic}} because there are 360 degrees in any complete revolution (circle), which takes about 365 days in the case of one revolution of the Earth around the Sun. Any given "distant" star in the belt of the ecliptic will be visible at night for only half of the year, when it will always remain below the horizon. During the other half of the year it will appear to be above the horizon but not visible because the sunlight is too bright during the day. The star's heliacal rising will occur when the Earth has moved to a point in its orbit where the star appears on the eastern horizon at dawn. Each day after the heliacal rising, the star will rise slightly earlier and remain visible for longer before the light from the rising sun overwhelms it. Over the following days the star will move further and further westward (about one degree per day) relative to the Sun, until eventually it is no longer visible in the sky at sunrise because it has already set below the western horizon. This is called the ''acronycal setting''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.sizes.com/time/risesetstar.htm|title=rising and setting of stars|website=www.sizes.com}} The same star will reappear in the eastern sky at dawn approximately one year after its previous heliacal rising. For stars near the {{ill|Ecliptic|en|Ecliptic|qid=Q79852|lt=Ecliptic|1=Ecliptic}}, the small difference between the [[:en:Solar year|solar]] and {{ill|Sidereal year|en|Sidereal year|qid=Q2281928|lt=Sidereal year|1=Sidereal year}}s due to {{ill|axial precession|en|axial precession|qid=Q83094|lt=axial precession|1=Axial precession}} will cause their heliacal rising to recur about one {{ill|Sidereal year|en|Sidereal year|qid=Q2281928|lt=Sidereal year|1=Sidereal year}} (about 365.2564 days) later, though this depends on its {{ill|proper motion|en|proper motion|qid=Q193223|lt=proper motion|1=Proper motion}}. For stars far from the ecliptic, the period is somewhat different and varies slowly, but in any case the heliacal rising will move all the way through the {{ill|Zodiac|en|Zodiac|qid=Q40540|lt=Zodiac|1=Zodiac}} in about 26,000 years due to [[:en:precession of the equinoxes|precession of the equinoxes]]. Because the heliacal rising depends on the observation of the object, its exact timing can be dependent on weather conditions.{{Cite web|url=https://archaeoastronomy.wordpress.com/2005/09/10/archaic-astronomy-and-heliacal-rising/|title=Archaic Astronomy and Heliacal Rising|date=September 10, 2005}} Heliacal phenomena and their use throughout history have made them useful points of reference in [[:en:archeoastronomy|archeoastronomy]].{{cite journal | last=Schaefer | first=Bradley E. | title=Heliacal Rise Phenomena | journal=Journal for the History of Astronomy | publisher=SAGE Publications | volume=18 | issue=11 | year=1987 | issn=0021-8286 | doi=10.1177/002182868701801103 | pages=S19–S33| s2cid=116923139 }} == Non-application to circumpolar stars == Some stars, when viewed from {{ill|Latitude|en|Latitude|qid=Q34027|lt=Latitude|1=Latitude}}s not at the {{ill|equator|en|equator|qid=Q23538|lt=equator|1=Equator}}, do not rise or set. These are {{ill|circumpolar star|en|circumpolar star|qid=Q12115|lt=circumpolar star|1=Circumpolar star}}s, which are either always in the sky or never. For example, the [[:en:North Star|North Star]] (Polaris) is not visible in Australia and the [[:en:Southern Cross|Southern Cross]] is not seen in Europe, because they always stay below the respective horizons. The term ''circumpolar'' is somewhat localised as between the Tropic of Cancer and the Equator, the Southern polar constellations have a brief spell of annual visibility (thus "heliacal" rising and "cosmic" setting) and the same applies as to the other polar constellations in respect of the reverse tropic. == History == Constellations containing stars that rise and set were incorporated into early {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=Calendar|1=Calendar}}s or {{ill|Zodiac|en|Zodiac|qid=Q40540|lt=Zodiac|1=Zodiac}}s. The {{ill|Sumer|en|Sumer|qid=Q35355|lt=Sumer|1=Sumer}}ians, {{ill|Babylonia|en|Babylonia|qid=Q47690|lt=Babylonia|1=Babylonia}}ns, [[:en:Ancient Egyptians|Egyptians]], and {{ill|Ancient Greece|en|Ancient Greece|qid=Q11772|lt=Greeks|1=Ancient Greece}} all used the heliacal risings of various stars for the timing of agricultural activities. Because of its position about 40° off the ecliptic, the heliacal risings of the bright star {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sirius|1=Sirius}} in {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}} occurred not over a period of exactly one {{ill|Sidereal year|en|Sidereal year|qid=Q2281928|lt=Sidereal year|1=Sidereal year}} but over a period called the "[[Sothic year]]" (from "Sothis", the name for the star Sirius). The Sothic year was about a minute longer than a [[:en:Julian year (calendar)|Julian year]] of 365.25 days.{{harvp|Tetley|2014|p=[https://web.archive.org/web/20171114202029/http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_3_investigating_ancient_egyptian_calendars.pdf 42]}}. Since the development of {{ill|Civilization|en|Civilization|qid=Q8432|lt=Civilization|1=Civilization}}, this has occurred at {{ill|Cairo|en|Cairo|qid=Q85|lt=Cairo|1=Cairo}} approximately on July 19 on the {{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|qid=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar}}.{{citation |contribution-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20131225071503/https://www.lavia.org/english/archivo/egyptiancalendaren.html|contribution=Ancient Egyptian Civil Calendar |title = La Via |url = http://www.lavia.org/indexIT.html |access-date=8 February 2017 }}.{{efn|The exact date varies with {{ill|Latitude|en|Latitude|qid=Q34027|lt=Latitude|1=Latitude}}, so that Sirius's return is observed about 8–10 days later on the [[Mediterranean Sea|Mediterranean]] coast than at {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|qid=Q284009|lt=Aswan|1=Elephantine}}.{{citation |last=Tetley |first = M. Christine |title = The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings, ''Vol. I'' |url = http://www.egyptchronology.com/vols-1--2.html |year = 2014 |page = [http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_3_investigating_ancient_egyptian_calendars.pdf 43] |access-date=2017-02-09 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170211075421/http://www.egyptchronology.com/vols-1--2.html |archive-date=2017-02-11 |url-status=dead }}. Official observations were made at {{ill|Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|en|Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)|qid=Q191687|lt=Heliopolis|1=Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)}} or {{ill|Memphis, Egypt|en|Memphis, Egypt|qid=Q5715|lt=Memphis|1=Memphis, Egypt}} near {{ill|Cairo|en|Cairo|qid=Q85|lt=Cairo|1=Cairo}}, {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|qid=Q101583|lt=Thebes|1=Thebes, Egypt}}, and {{ill|Elephantine|en|Elephantine|qid=Q284009|lt=Elephantine|1=Elephantine}} near {{ill|Aswan|en|Aswan|qid=Q29888|lt=Aswan|1=Aswan}}. The date at any location also slowly varies within the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} by about three days every four centuries. July 19 of the Julian Calendar occurs on August 1 Gregorian in the 20th and 21st centuries.}} Its returns also roughly corresponded to the onset of the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|qid=Q1519552|lt=annual flooding|1=Flooding of the Nile}} of the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|qid=Q3392|lt=Nile|1=Nile}}, although the flooding is based on the tropical year and so would occur about three quarters of a day earlier per century in the Julian or Sothic year. (July 19, 1000 BC in the Julian Calendar is July 10 in the [[:en:proleptic Gregorian Calendar|proleptic Gregorian Calendar]]. At that time, the sun would be somewhere near {{ill|Regulus|en|Regulus|qid=Q76493786|lt=Regulus|1=Regulus}} in {{ill|Leo (constellation)|en|Leo (constellation)|qid=Q8853|lt=Leo|1=Leo (constellation)}}, where it is around August 21 in the 2020s.) The ancient Egyptians appear to have constructed their 365-day {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|qid=Q254101|lt=civil calendar|1=Egyptian calendar}} at a time when [[:en:Wep Renpet (month)|Wep Renpet]], its {{ill|New Year|en|New Year|qid=Q34812|lt=New Year|1=New Year}}, corresponded with Sirius's return to the night sky. Although this calendar's lack of {{ill|Leap year|en|Leap year|qid=Q19828|lt=Leap year|1=Leap year}}s caused the event to shift one day every four years or so, {{ill|Egyptian astronomy|en|Egyptian astronomy|qid=Q751843|lt=astronomical records|1=Egyptian astronomy}} of this displacement led to the discovery of the [[Sothic cycle]] and, later, the establishment of the more accurate {{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|qid=Q11184|lt=Julian|1=Julian calendar}} and [[:en:Alexandrian calendar|Alexandrian calendar]]s. The Egyptians also devised a method of telling the time at night based on the heliacal risings of 36 [[:en:Decans|decan stars]], one for each 10° segment of the 360° circle of the zodiac and corresponding to the ten-day "weeks" of their civil calendar. To the {{ill|Māori people|en|Māori people|qid=Q6122670|lt=Māori|1=Māori people}} of {{ill|New Zealand|en|New Zealand|qid=Q664|lt=New Zealand|1=New Zealand}}, the {{ill|Pleiades|en|Pleiades|qid=Q13423|lt=Pleiades|1=Pleiades}} are called {{ill|Matariki|en|Matariki|qid=Q455596|lt=Matariki|1=Matariki}}, and their heliacal rising signifies the beginning of the new year (around June). The {{ill|Mapuche|en|Mapuche|qid=Q178484|lt=Mapuche|1=Mapuche}} of {{ill|South America|en|South America|qid=Q18|lt=South America|1=South America}} called the Pleiades ''Ngauponi'' which in the vicinity of the ''[[we tripantu]]'' (Mapuche new year) will disappear by the west, ''lafkenmapu'' or ''ngulumapu'', appearing at dawn to the East, a few days before the birth of new life in nature. Heliacal rising of Ngauponi, i.e. appearance of the Pleiades by the horizon over an hour before the sun approximately 12 days before the winter solstice, announced ''we tripantu''. When a planet has a heliacal rising, there is a {{ill|Conjunction (astronomy)|en|Conjunction (astronomy)|qid=Q191536|lt=conjunction|1=Conjunction (astronomy)}} with the sun beforehand. Depending on the type of conjunction, there may be a {{ill|Syzygy (astronomy)|en|Syzygy (astronomy)|qid=Q13567909|lt=syzygy|1=Syzygy (astronomy)}}, {{ill|eclipse|en|eclipse|qid=Q141022|lt=eclipse|1=Eclipse}}, [[:en:Transit (astronomy)|transit]], or {{ill|occultation|en|occultation|qid=Q202746|lt=occultation|1=Occultation}} of the sun. ==Acronycal and cosmic(al)== The rising of a planet above the eastern horizon at {{ill|Sunset|en|Sunset|qid=Q166564|lt=Sunset|1=Sunset}} is called its ''[[Wiktionary:acronycal|acronycal]] rising'', which for a {{ill|superior planet|en|superior planet|qid=Q844911|lt=superior planet|1=Superior planet}} signifies an [[:en:opposition (planets)|opposition]], another type of {{ill|Syzygy (astronomy)|en|Syzygy (astronomy)|qid=Q13567909|lt=syzygy|1=Syzygy (astronomy)}}. When the Moon has an acronycal rising, it will occur near {{ill|Full moon|en|Full moon|qid=Q104641|lt=Full moon|1=Full moon}} and thus, two or three times a year, a noticeable {{ill|lunar eclipse|en|lunar eclipse|qid=Q44235|lt=lunar eclipse|1=Lunar eclipse}}. ''[[Wiktionary:cosmical|Cosmic(al)]]'' can refer to rising with sunrise or setting at sunset, or the first setting at morning twilight.{{Cite journal|url=https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2012AAS...21915001H|title=Acronical Risings and Settings|first=Thomas A.|last=Hockey|journal=American Astronomical Society Meeting Abstracts #219 |date=January 1, 2012|volume=219|pages=150.01|bibcode=2012AAS...21915001H |via=NASA ADS}} Risings and settings are furthermore differentiated between ''apparent'' (the above discussed) and actual or ''true'' risings or settings. ===Overview=== The use of the terms cosmical and acronycal is not consistent. The following table gives an overview of the different application of the terms to the rising and setting instances. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Daytime ! Visibility ! Rising (east) ! Setting (west) |- | rowspan="2" |Morning (matutinal) | True (in daylight) | Cosmical | Acronycal/Cosmical |- | Apparent (in twilight) | '''Heliacal
(first {{ill|night sky|en|night sky|qid=Q1153471|lt=night sky|1=Night sky}} appearance)''' | Heliacal/Cosmical
(last morning appearance) |- | rowspan="2" |Evening (vesper) | True (in daylight) | Acronycal | Cosmical/Acronycal |- | Apparent (in twilight) | Heliacal/Acronycal
(first evening appearance) | Heliacal
(last night sky appearance) |- | colspan="4" |{{cite journal | last=Robinson | first=Matthew | title=Ardua et Astra: On the Calculation of the Dates of the Rising and Setting of Stars | journal=Classical Philology | publisher=University of Chicago Press | volume=104 | issue=3 | year=2009 | issn=0009-837X | doi=10.1086/650145 | pages=354–375| s2cid=161711710 | url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/4840/ }}{{cite web | title=Understanding - Rising and setting of stars | website=Encyclopedia FP7 ESPaCE | url=https://promenade.imcce.fr/en/pages6/725.html | access-date=2022-10-29}} |} ==See also== *{{ill|Dog days|en|Dog days|qid=Q1065821|lt=Dog days|1=Dog days}} *[[:en:Steering star|Steering star]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{Wikisource1911Enc|Heliacal}} {{Reflist}} {{Portal bar|Astronomy|Stars|Spaceflight|Outer space|Solar System}} {{translated page|de|Heliacal rising|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[en:Heliacal rising]] [[ar:شروق نجمي]] [[bg:Хелиакален изгрев]] [[ca:Ortus helíac]] [[de:Heliakisch]] [[el:Ηλιακή ανατολή]] [[eo:Helioapero]] [[es:Orto helíaco]] [[fa:کنار روزی]] [[fi:Heliakkinen nousu]] [[fr:Lever héliaque]] [[ga:Éirí héileacach]] [[gl:Orto helíaco]] [[ig:Uto Heliacal]] [[it:Levata eliaca]] [[ja:ヒライアカル・ライジング]] [[ko:신출]] [[la:Ortus et occasus heliacus]] [[nn:Heliakisk oppgang]] [[no:Heliakisk oppgang]] [[pl:Wschód heliakalny]] [[pt:Nascer helíaco]] [[ro:Răsărit heliacal]] [[ru:Гелиакический восход]] [[sk:Heliaktický východ hviezdy]] [[sv:Heliakisk uppgång]] [[ta:விண்மீன் எழுச்சி]] [[uk:Геліактичний схід]] [[vi:Mọc cùng Mặt Trời]] [[zh:偕日升]] {{wikidata link|Q368373}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Technical factors of astrology]] [[Category:Time in astronomy]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]
